left.title: Darline Graham, Lindsey Graham’s sister, to run for full Senate term

left-center.title: Lindsey Graham’s Sister, Darline Graham, Says She Will Run to Succeed Him

center.title: 'I'm in.' Darline Graham says she will run for a full term in Lindsey Graham's Senate seat

right.title: Darline Graham Says She'll Run for Full 6-Year Term

farLeftText:
Darline Graham, the South Carolina interim senator and sister of the late Lindsey Graham, said she plans to run for a full Senate term after Donald Trump endorsed her last week.

In an interview with Sean Hannity that will air in full on Monday, Graham – who was appointed by Governor Henry McMaster to finish the remainder of her brother’s term – said she had “made a decision”.

“I’m in,” she told the Fox News anchor.

Graham, 62, made history as the first woman to represent South Carolina in the Senate when she was sworn in last week.

Her brother, Lindsey died earlier this month, aged 71, after what his office called a “brief and sudden illness”. The chief medical examiner preliminarily ruled that he died of aortic dissection due to cardiovascular disease.

On Friday, Trump said he had encouraged Darline Graham to run for a full six-year term representing South Carolina.

“I asked Darline, for the Good of our Nation, to run for the U.S. Senate in the Special Republican Primary,” he wrote on Truth Social. “Darline, who comes from an absolutely incredible family, has been a WINNER all of her life and, should she accept, has my Complete and Total Endorsement.”

Because Lindsey Graham won the Republican nomination last month, the state will now hold a special primary to replace him on the ballot on 11 August.
centerLeftText:
Senator Darline Graham of South Carolina, who was sworn in last week to temporarily replace her brother, the late Senator Lindsey Graham, said on Monday that she would enter the race to succeed him.

The announcement came days after President Trump urged her to run for the seat. It cements her rapid transformation from a grieving sister seen as a caretaker of the Republican-held Senate seat to a first-time candidate vying for a place in the nation’s most powerful governing body.

Ms. Graham shared her decision in an interview with the Fox News host Sean Hannity.

“I feel like I can do it,” she said in a clip from the interview, which was set to air on Monday night. “I feel an inner peace about it.”

The filing period for the special Republican primary election opens on Tuesday and closes on July 28, the day of Mr. Graham’s funeral in Washington. The special election is scheduled for Aug. 11.
centerText: "I've made a decision," Darline Graham told Fox News Channel host Sean Hannity , according to a clip released of an interview set to air later Monday. "I'm in." The entrance of Darline Graham — sworn in last week to fulfill the remaining months of her late brother's term, which expires in January — into the Republican scramble to select a new Senate nominee further complicates an already rushed process following Lindsey Graham 's death earlier this month. A weeklong filing period opens Tuesday for the special Republican primary, which will be Aug. 11. On Friday, President Donald Trump said that Darline Graham had his "Complete and Total Endorsement" to seek the nomination, adding, "RUN, DARLINE, RUN!" READ MORE: Who is Darline Graham, the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's sister? Last week, Gov. Henry McMaster appointed Darline Graham to serve the remainder of her brother's term, which ends in January. In his announcement, McMaster made no reference to Darline Graham as a placeholder or symbolic appointment, although a person familiar with McMaster's thinking but unauthorized to speak publicly said the governor, in selecting Darline Graham, had never contemplated that she would run for the seat herself. Before the emergence of Darline Graham — and their Oval Office meeting after she was sworn in — Trump had previously suggested he could back a potential candidacy from Rep. Russell Fry, who said Monday he would officially enter the special primary. Several other noteworthy politicians, including Rep. Ralph Norman, have already gotten in, and others like Rep. Nancy Mace and Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette have been eyeing a run. Watch the clip in the player above. Darline Graham told Hannity said she had been "praying a lot" about how to navigate her new role. "It is a tremendous amount of pressure," she said. "I know I'm a hard worker. I learned that from Lindsey," Darline Graham said. "I feel like I can do it. I feel an inner peace about it. Will it be difficult? Yes." READ MORE: Darline Graham's career centered disability. Now she could help shape national policy Funeral services for Lindsey Graham are scheduled to be held next week in Washington and South Carolina. A preliminary report from the medical examiner said he suffered a tear in his aorta. The millions in Lindsey Graham's campaign account aren't funds that Darline Graham could directly access in a run of her own, according to Bradley A. Smith, a former chairman of the Federal Election Commission. Under federal rules, Lindsey Graham's campaign would be limited to transferring just $2,000 to a potential Darline Graham candidacy. However, Smith said there is no limit on how much it could transfer to the National Republican Senatorial Committee, which could — thanks to a Supreme Court decision last month — "spend an unlimited amount in coordination with Darline's campaign."
centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! As the field for the race to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham takes shape, one prominent lawmaker is declining to dive into the arena hours after the contest was shaken up. Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., opted against a bid after she strongly suggested that she’d throw her hat into the arena on Monday in a video statement. Last August, Mace launched a campaign for the Republican gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Henry McMaster, but she finished a disappointing fifth out of five major candidates in last month’s Republican primary. "I want to be straight with you, I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now," Mace said. "I'm not chasing another title, I'm chasing justice, justice for me, justice for other women and justice for every little girl who was told to stay silent," she continued. "That's where my focus is today, and that's where it's going to stay." FOX NEWS CHANNEL’S 'HANNITY' TO AIR EXCLUSIVE FIRST INTERVIEW WITH SEN DARLINE GRAHAM ON MONDAY Her decision followed a move by Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., to take up her brother's mantle after she was appointed to finish off the remainder of her late brother's term. She made her decision to officially enter the upcoming special election after a push from President Donald Trump. Graham, during an interview with Fox News’ Sean Hannity, acknowledged the "tremendous amount of pressure" to take over the position and run. "I feel like I can do it, I feel an inner peace about it," Graham said. "Will it be difficult? Yes." When asked if she had made a decision on what she would do, she told Hannity she had. "I'm in," Graham said. Her addition to the growing roster of hopefuls to replace the late lawmaker will likely shake up the race less than one day before candidates can begin officially filing to run for the nomination. Graham will come up against other challengers in her bid to claim the GOP nomination. Reps. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., Russell Fry, R-S.C., and businessman Mark Lynch, have all publicly committed to running. And as the filing deadline fast approaches, both Mace and Norman turned their steel against their colleague, Fry, in what’s shaping up to be a messy, Palmetto State brawl. Mace argued that should Fry win the nomination, and subsequently the general election, he would vacate his "Republican House seat until next May." REPUBLICANS DEFY TRUMP, JUMP INTO SOUTH CAROLINA SENATE RACE DESPITE HIS HANDPICKED SUCCESSOR "That narrows an already razor-thin majority and puts the Speaker's gavel in play," Mace said. "I will never hand Hakeem Jeffries that gift." "If you're running for two offices at once, you've already told us who you are," she continued. "That's not about politics anymore — that's about power. You're either with the people you serve or you're not." Fry announced Monday morning that he would enter the race, hours after Norman tossed in his hat, too. Mace has been making public overtures that she wants to jump in, but has so far not made anything official. A source in Fry’s political orbit told Fox News Digital that the lawmaker has "accomplished more than all the potential candidates combined," having passed three bills in his first four years in Congress. "This is really about giving voters a choice and not a coronation," they said. Still, the conservative duo are going for blood against Fry, arguing that his bid for the seat is purely politically motivated and jeopardizes the House’s already slim majority heading into the midterm elections later this year. Under South Carolina law, Fry can appear for multiple offices on the ballot. He was unopposed for his primary election for South Carolina’s 7th congressional district, and if he wins the nomination, he’ll be on the ballot twice for both. TRUMP REVEALS WHO HE'S EYEING TO REPLACE LINDSEY GRAHAM Ultimately, he’ll have to choose if he wins both, which would trigger another special election to fill his place. The top two Republicans in the South Carolina Statehouse, South Carolina Senate President Thomas Alexander and South Carolina House Speaker Murrell Smith, said in a statement that the upcoming special election has "highlighted issues in South Carolina law," and noted that, "In the coming weeks, we intend to address these important issues so that we can provide the process available for our citizens." That is not a problem for either Mace nor Norman, who both ran and failed to clinch the Republican gubernatorial nomination last month. "SC Republican voters soundly rejected Nancy and Ralph as recently as last month because conservatives are tired of career politicians who care more about their own social media than getting actual results for families," South Carolina state Sen. JD Chaplin, and a spokesperson for Fry's campaign, told Fox News Digital. Norman was less venomous in his take on the situation, and noted on X that he respected Fry and had enjoyed his time serving with him in the House. Still, his candidacy, he argued, "could leave his seat vacant until May 18th." "This would leave his constituents unrepresented, jeopardize President Trump’s agenda, and put the House at risk of being led by a Speaker Hakeem Jeffries," Norman said. "We can’t let that happen!!" CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Senate Majority Leader Sen. John Thune, speaking to reporters on Monday afternoon, referred to Darlene Graham and said, "She obviously carries on Lindsey's legacy. But I think she's a very accomplished person in her own right." Asked if he would be endorsing her, the longtime lawmaker from South Dakota and top Republican in the Senate said, "I'm not endorsing anyone at the moment." Fox News' Tyler Olson contributed to this report

centerText:
US Vice-President JD Vance has announced the birth of his fourth child, a boy, with his wife Usha Vance.

Alec Neel Vance was born on Sunday morning, the vice-president said in a statement on social media.

“Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother,” he added, also thanking the military doctors and staff at Walter Reed Medical Center for their care.

It is the first time a baby has been born to a sitting vice-president while in office for more than 150 years, according to the Associated Press. President Donald Trump hailed the news, writing in a post: "Congratulations! A perfect baby boy for the wonderful Vance family."

The pair have three other children: Ewan, 9, Vivek, 6, and four-year-old Mirabel.

Usha Vance (née Chilukuri) was born and raised in San Diego, California, to a mechanical engineer father and a molecular biologist mother who had moved to the US from Andhra Pradesh, India.

She met JD Vance as a student at Yale Law School in 2010, when they joined a discussion group on "social decline in white America".

farRightText:
Vice President JD Vance and his wife, Usha Vance, announced the birth of their fourth child on Sunday, making history as the first sitting vice president to welcome a child in 150 years.

The announcement welcomed the arrival of Alec Neel Vance on Sunday, the fourth sibling of Ewan, 9; Vivek, 6; and Mirabel, 4.

“We are excited to announce that our baby boy, Alec Neel Vance, was born this morning. Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother,” the statement said.

Vance thanked the White House medical team along with doctors and staff at the Walter Reed National Military Medical Center.

As noted by the Associated Press, Vance became the first vice president since the 1800s have a child while serving as the second-highest official in the United States.

“Schuyler Colfax and his second wife, Ellen Wade, had a son, Schuyler Colfax III, in 1870 when Colfax was serving as vice president, according to the White House Historical Association,” it noted. “Decades before that, John C. Calhoun and his wife, Floride Bonneau, had a son, William, in 1829 when Calhoun was vice president, the association said.”

Colfax served under President Ulysses S. Grant between 1869-1873.

Several members of the Trump administration have welcomed babies during the president’s second term, including White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt.

topic: Save Struggling Swimmer Ohio River Attempt

left.title: Five people die in attempt to save struggling swimmer in Ohio river

center.title: 5 dead after group attempted to save a struggling swimmer in an Ohio river

right-center.title: 5 dead in attempt to save a struggling swimmer in Ohio river

farLeftText:
Five people have died in a river in Ohio after several of them went into the water to help another person who was struggling to swim, authorities said Monday.

A motorist called 911 after spotting a frightened child who had been with the group and was running on a nearby road, Delaware county sheriff Jeffrey Balzer said at a news conference on Monday.

Two children under age 10 have been placed in the care of family services, the sheriff said, including the child who ran for help.

The group had gathered on Sunday along the Scioto River in the Columbus suburb of Powell when one of them went into the river to go swimming.

“And that’s when the tragedy started to occur,” Balzer said, as the others who jumped in to help also began struggling.

Rescue crews searching the water on Sunday found two women, who were pronounced dead at a hospital, Balzer said. The bodies of three men were found in the river on Monday, said Tracy Whited, a spokesperson for the Delaware county sheriff’s department.

The tragedy occurred two days after five members of a family were killed in southern Utah after a flash flood swept through canyons during a hiking trip.

centerRightText:
NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles!

An attempt to save a struggling swimmer in an Ohio river left five people dead over the weekend, authorities said Monday .

A group gathered Sunday evening at the Scioto River in Powell, a Columbus suburb, when one of them went into the water to go swimming.

"And that’s when the tragedy started to occur," Delaware County Sheriff Jeffrey Balzer said at a news conference Monday. Others then jumped into the river in an attempt to help the swimmer.

"I understand that one person got in the river, was struggling in the water. Two people went in to try to save them," Balzer said. Those people started struggling in the river, Now we have two additional people go in the water."

OFF-DUTY ICE OFFICER RELIES ON 'SIXTH SENSE' TO SAVE DROWNING CHILD BEFORE DELIVERING MESSAGE TO CRITICS

At around 9 p.m., a "distraught" child with the group ran onto a nearby road and was spotted by a motorist who called 911. The child said his family was in the river, Balzer said.

First responders arrived and found two women, who were pronounced dead at a hospital, Balzer said.

The bodies of three men were found in the river on Monday, Tracy Whited, a spokesperson for the Delaware County Sheriff’s Department, told The Associated Press. The identities of the deceased have not been released.

No boat was at the scene and it was unclear if alcohol played a factor in the tragedy, the sheriff said.

CARNIVAL CRUISE PASSENGER, 67, FOUND DEAD WHILE SNORKELING OFF THE COAST OF AUSTRALIA DURING EXCURSION

Two children under the age of 10 that were part of the group have been placed in the care of family services, said Balzer.

The sheriff urged the public to have safety devices like life vests on hand when gathering near bodies of water.

The 230-mile Scioto River is a popular area for locals and boasts fish populations of bass, saugeye, catfish, and carp, according to publiclands.com.

The deaths happened days after five family members were killed in Utah after a flash flood swept through the region.

centerLeftText: Ontario Premier Doug Ford on Monday slammed President Donald Trump’s recent attacks on Canada, and urged the U.S. leader to consider sending wildfire help rather than lobbing threats at his northern neighbor. “I get frustrated, just listening to...a bunch of rubbish,” said Ford. “With all the assets the U.S. has, he’s sitting there complaining,” Ford added. “Why don’t you send up some water bombers? Why don’t you start helping us, like we would help you in a heartbeat?” Trump has repeatedly criticized Canada in the last week and mused about levying more tariffs on the country as a so-called punishment for wildfire smoke. Hours after Ford’s remarks, Trump imposed a staggering 50% tariff on numerous Canadian goods, citing claims of discrimination against American products. Canada has historically provided aid when the U.S. was experiencing severe wildfires, just as Ford noted. In 2025, Canada sent equipment and dozens of firefighters to help combat blazes raging in parts of southern California. “You should support your neighbors; that’s my message to the president,” Ford said. The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
centerText: In our news wrap Monday, Andy Burnham became Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade, Andrew and Tristan Tate will remain in federal custody as they fight extradition to the UK where they face dozens of new rape and sex trafficking charges and protests in New Delhi turned violent after police intervened in a student-led demonstration calling for reforms to India's education system.
centerRightText: The United Kingdom has yet another prime minister after Andy Burnham was appointed to the position by King Charles on Monday morning. He is the seventh prime minister in the last decade. Burnham, the former mayor of Greater Manchester, whose nickname is the "King of the North," was elected as the leader of the Labour Party on Friday, just four weeks after being parachuted back into Parliament at the Makerfield special election. Burnham has long wanted to be the Labour leader, having put himself forward twice before for the job. However, he was only able to challenge Keir Starmer as a sitting member of Parliament (M.P.) after the former prime minister announced his resignation less than a month ago. With other potential replacements bowing out of the race, Burnham was able to become Labour leader, and by consequence, British prime minister, without a contest. Partly for this reason, little is known about how he will govern. Still, his acceptance speech after becoming leader of the Labour Party outlined some of his political vision. The M.P. promised to be "pro-business," "problem-solving rather than point-scoring," and "authentically…Labour." He seemed to signal that he would pursue a more interventionist, decentralizing, and explicitly post-Thatcherite government. On Monday, upon becoming prime minister, Burnham promised "a new political model and a new economic model," and railed against former Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher's free market reforms. "In the 1980s, Britain took some wrong turns. Political power was centralized. Economic power privatized. Large parts of the country de-industrialized, and they still haven't recovered," he said. "Many feel as though they're still in decline and they don't have the ability to turn things around. And that's why we will change politics to make it more collaborative, more about problem-solving than point-scoring." "We will take power out of here and carry it into every postcode in the land so that they can do more. And in doing more, build a new economy where we put life's essentials back under stronger public control to make them affordable to you again, reindustrializing Britain using public procurement to back British industry." This call for more government control was expected. During his acceptance speech on Friday, he asked: "If we don't have sufficient public control over the cost of the essentials, how can we have control over inflation, public spending, and the rest of the economy?" Although there are no specific details of what this greater "control" could look like, he has previously said that the nationalization of Thames Water—which provides water to London and the surrounding region—"is absolutely an option." However, a group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to threaten legal action if nationalization is pursued. Burnham has also promised a whole-of-government strategy to address homelessness. "I will soon go through that door behind me and issue my first instruction: to end rough sleeping in our country," Burnham said on Monday. He made a similar pledge as mayor of Manchester, where he vowed to end rough sleeping by 2020. Millions of pounds of taxpayer cash were spent on his "A Bed Every Night" program. Instead, official numbers of homelessness rose in four consecutive years, and more than doubled from 2021 to 2025. Perhaps "stronger public control" is not the solution here, but speeding up the construction of private housing. There was also speculation that Burnham would announce plans for new oil and gas drilling in the North Sea, drawing praise from U.S. President Donald Trump on Truth Social : "The People of Aberdeen, in Scotland, are dancing in the streets because the new Prime Minister, Andy Burnham, has stated that he will be opening up, all the way, the invaluable North Sea Oil!" However, the Financial Times reported that the ban on new licenses would remain in place, with Deputy Labour Leader Lucy Powell describing the government's approach as a "change of emphasis" on North Sea oil and gas. Not all of what we know so far of Burnham's policies appears to be big government. Over the weekend, he said he would scrap Starmer's digital ID program, which would have created a centralized government database linking people's identities to verified personal information and their access to public services. Big Brother Watch warned of the "serious privacy and security risks" associated with the government's proposal to introduce mandatory digital ID, and #Together said such a move would turn Britain into a "Papers Please" country. A Burnham spokesperson told the BBC that the "time and resource that was going to be spent on a national ID scheme will go instead to where it's most needed, such as helping with the cost of living." Burnham has also signaled that he won't introduce a wealth tax immediately. However, his promises to increase government control in the economy suggest that the associated costs will be found somewhere, and tax rises and increased government borrowing are likely to be a part of that. Of course, more borrowing and higher taxes are unlikely to fix the largest issue that Burnham needs to address: The British economy, which grew by a meager 0.6 percent in the first quarter of this year (embarrassing compared to the U.S.' 2.1 percent). Twenty-five percent of working-age people are out of work, and those who are working are set to pay the highest tax burden in British history. Youth unemployment is soaring, with the number of young people aged 16 to 24 who are NEETs (Not in Employment, Education, or Training) increasing to 13.5 percent—more than one million people—in the first quarter of this year. Consequently, Britain is now poorer than America's poorest state. Andy Burnham enters No. 10 with Britain in a dire state, driven by years of increases in public spending, tax hikes, and crippling red tape. Given his promise to increase government control, there is little reason to believe he will turn the tide.
farRightText: Veteran Westminster swamp creature turned ‘King of the North’ political chameleon Andy Burnham has been given the keys to Number 10 Downing Street, officially taking over the office of Prime Minister on Monday after Sir Keir Starmer was forced to resign just over two years after taking power. The London political churn has spat out another one. Sir Keir Starmer formally resigned as Prime Minister to make way for former Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, who became the seventh prime minister in a decade, and fifth to take power in less than four years. While Burnham presents as a more personable figure than his predecessor, it remains to be seen how far northern charm will take him and if he can deliver on the key priorities of the British public. Starmer attempted to hold his head high on Monday morning as he left Downing Street — as opposed to the tearful resignation announcement he delivered last month — claiming that he left the country in a “stronger and fairer” position than it was when he came into office. This was clearly not the opinion of the British public, who delivered Starmer’s Labour Party an electoral drubbing in the local ‘mid-term’ elections in May in favour of Brexit boss Nigel Farage’s Reform UK party. The defeat came in the wake of his government failing to return the British economy to growth while imposing the highest tax burden on the nation since the Second World War and having failed to protect the country’s borders, with over 70,000 illegals crossing the English Channel under his watch, the most of any modern prime minister. The Starmer government was marred from the start, having bungled the response to the Southport massacre, in which he appeared to have more rage toward the so-called “far-right” rioters than towards the Rwandan-heritage teen who stabbed three young girls to death and left ten more injured at a Taylor Swift dance party just days into his term. However, the drumbeat call for his resignation truly ramped up last autumn following the release of the Epstein files by the Trump administration in the United States, which revealed that Starmer’s ambassador to Washington, Peter ‘Prince of Darkness’ Mandelson, had continued his close relationship with the New York financier long after he was convicted of his first child prostitution offence. Sir Keir attempted to limp on, throwing his longtime political ally, Chief of Staff, and architect of his rise, Morgan McSweeney, to the wolves. Yet his continued presence as Prime Minister appeared more of a function of no challenger being foolish to take over the job — and therefore the blame — before the expected heavy losses at the May elections, shortly after which self-described socialist Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham finally put himself forward for the top job. This morning, Burnham met with King Charles III for the traditional “kissing hands” ceremony, in which the British Monarch officially invited Burnham to form a government in his name. In his first address to the nation as Prime Minister outside Number 10, Burnham acknowledged the political turmoil that led to his rise, saying: “I am acutely conscious that I am… the seventh Prime Minister since 2016. Making this a moment for reflection, and new resolution… it requires my generation of politicians to raise our game, and rise to the challenge. Britain needs to show the world we can regain our stability once again.” In his speech, the new PM pitched himself as an economic reformer, promising to bring prosperity from London to the rest of the country. “We will take power out of here and carry it into every postcode in the land, so that they can do more, and in doing more, build a new economy where we put life’s essentials back under stronger public control to make them affordable to you again,” he said. Despite his folksy act, having taken the moniker of ‘King of the North’, following his nearly decade in office as the Mayor of Greater Manchester, Burnham appeared to have consciously concocted such an image after spending the prior decade as a Westminster insider under the governments of former Labour PMs Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. His rise to the highest office in the land over the past month also had the distinct odour of swamp. To put forward a leadership challenge against Starmer, Burnham needed a seat in the House of Commons, and the constituency of Makerfield was conveniently vacated by anti-Breitbart activist MP Josh Simons, who himself had been forced out of the Starmer government over scandals involving his pro-censorship activities at the Labour Together think tank founded by Starmer’s former Chief of Staff Morgan McSweeney. Simons, who appeared outside of Downing Street on Monday morning’s coronation for Burnham, is tipped to be set for a top gig in the new government in exchange for his move to give up his seat in Makerfield for the new PM. His return to power will likely signal continued crackdowns on social media, potentially including a ban on VPNs, as the government seeks to ban under-16s from accessing social media. Burnham’s victory in Makerfield last month was also facilitated, in part, by the help of the far-left cancel culture group HOPE Not Hate, which busied itself with digging up old social media posts of Burnham’s Reform opponent, and allegedly even leafleting on behalf of the Labour Party, for which Nigel Farage has launched a complaint at the Electoral Commission. Nevertheless, with just over 25,000 votes, Burnham was sent to Parliament and enabled to become Prime Minister. Yet, without having run on the 2024 Labour Party manifesto and only having been backed by a mere fraction of the public, Burnham is already facing calls for a general election from Farage and others, who argue that his administration has no popular mandate to govern. Burnham does not appear to have any interest in fighting a general election; indeed, he promised a Soviet-style 10-year plan to fix Britain’s economy, which he claimed was only faltering as a result of the policies put in place by Margaret Thatcher over four decades ago. “We have not been good enough and we need to be better. We will be. We will make this moment a circuit breaker for Britain, bringing forward the biggest changes in 40 years. A new political model, and a new economic model. In the 1980s, Britain took some wrong turns. Political power was centralised, economic power privatised. Large parts of the country, deindustrialised.” He vowed to “build a new economy where we put life’s essentials under stronger public control to make them affordable to you again. Reindustrialising Britain again with public procurement to back British industry. Later this year I will bring forward a new Plan for Britain. A ten year plan.” Burnham vowed to bring down the cost of living, build more state housing, end homeless sleeping, reform the education system with more mental health support, and claimed that he would reduce welfare spending while meeting international defence commitments. Yet with details lacking on Monday and his new government already signalling against new energy extraction from the oil-rich North Sea, it remains to be seen how such an industrial plan will fare. Urging his new counterpart in London to adopt his “drill baby, drill” ethos, U.S. President Trump urged Burnham to take advantage of Britain’s natural resources in the North Sea, saying that it could transform the UK from a “Poverty Stricken Disaster, to one of the Richest Countries anywhere in the World!” Time will tell if this Prime Minister will actually listen to such advice from across the pond, or if it will be more of the same from Burnham.

topic: Trump Impose Tariff Canadian Goods Donald

left.title: Donald Trump to impose 50% tariff on most Canadian goods, White House says

left-center.title: Trump to Impose 50% Tariff on Many Canadian Goods

farLeftText: Donald Trump is imposing 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods in response to the country retaliating against previous US tariffs, the White House announced Monday, declaring Canada has unfairly discriminated against American cars, alcohol and dairy products. The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement. The new tariffs will exclude energy products, fish, critical minerals and potash. They also exclude products already subject to tariffs aimed at protecting national security, such as steel and aluminum. The steep tariffs will probably unleash a new wave of economic chaos, with risks of higher inflation and further fraying of relations between two nations that had been closely woven together before Trump’s return to the White House. The White House said the tariffs will go into effect in 30 days, leaving time for possible negotiations between the two countries. Trump signed three proclamations to launch the tariffs under Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act. Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section because they said Trump could use it to destabilize the economy. A White House fact sheet claims Trump is “taking action to hold Canada accountable for its continued discrimination against and unreasonable and unequal treatment of U.S. commerce that has burdened and disadvantaged hardworking Americans”. Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American automobiles, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after the US president imposed tariffs on Canada under the pretext that it should do more to stop fentanyl smuggling into the US. Trump noted in his autos proclamation that Canada maintained, starting in April 2025, a 25% tariff on the imports of US motor vehicles that did not qualify for preferential treatment under the USMCA. The White House also cited all but two Canadian provinces and territories stopping the sale of US alcoholic beverages, while not imposing similar restrictions on other countries. Trump also objected to Canada’s treatment of US cheese, saying in his proclamation that Canada discriminates against the US compared to Europe on dairy products. Trump had threatened last week to impose tariffs due to smoke from devastating wildfires in Canada drifting across large portions of the US. “We are holding Canada responsible for the fact that they are not properly maintaining their Forests, and Brush therein, and the United States is being unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air, the quality of which is dangerous, and totally unacceptable!” he wrote on Truth Social. Trump watched Sunday’s World Cup final with Mark Carney, the Canadian prime minister who has previously pushed back on the US president and won the premiership last year on a platform of promising to stand up for Canada. After the match, Trump told reporters he had raised the issue of wildfire smoke with Carney. “I told him, I mean, ‘You got to stop these fires from coming in and, you know, poisoning our air. Our air has been poisoned,’” Trump said of their conversation. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion A White House official who previewed the new tariffs to reporters said Trump has requested aides explore additional tariffs due to Canadian wildfires harming US air quality, the Associated Press reported. The Canadian federal government did not immediately comment on Trump’s action, but Doug Ford, the Ontario premier, said he’d “never stop fighting to protect Ontario”. “If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Ford posted on social media. Candace Laing, the CEO of the Canadian chamber of commerce, said the Trump administration’s moves were “regrettable” but the two countries need to use the 30-day window before the tariffs start “to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks”. The new taxes on Canadian imports could carry serious political and economic risks for Trump ahead of the November midterm elections for control of Congress. His so-called “liberation day” tariffs last year in April provoked a financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation. In a 6-3 decision in February, the supreme court ruled that Trump had illegally used emergency executive power to impose his global tariffs, causing the administration to find alternative ways to raise import taxes based on a series of legal authorities. The US has been forced to pay back $81bn in tariffs so far this fiscal year. The Associated Press contributed reporting

topic: Launches 10Th Strikes Iran Round Fullscale

left.title: US launches 10th round of strikes as Iran says it is in ‘full-scale war’

left-center.title: U.S. launches 10th straight night of strikes on Iran as Pentagon reports nearly 100 injuries in July

farLeftText: The US military said it has launched its 10th consecutive round of strikes on Iran to force the reopening of the strait of Hormuz. Earlier on Monday, Iran’s president said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats said talks to de-escalate were continuing. Iranian media reported strikes around Tabriz, believed to hold underground missile bases, as well as Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power plant. The US military said it had struck “military command centres, air defence and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and communications networks”. Iran launched attacks against Bahrain, which hosts the US navy’s fifth fleet, and Kuwait, in addition to striking tankers in the strait of Hormuz, which carried a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. Bahrain said its air traffic systems were damaged by the strikes, but that traffic was not affected. The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen also announced a blockade of Saudi ports. “The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” the Iranian president, Masoud Pezeshkian, said in a statement on Monday, warning it might have to endure more strikes. US launches more airstrikes against Iran Meanwhile, US president Donald Trump signalled an escalation in tactics, saying on ​Monday ⁠that Iran ⁠would “pay” ​for killing US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in action over the ​weekend. The US military on Monday identified two soldiers killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is under way to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reported missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone. Since the war began, 17 US service members have been killed. Iran’s health ministry said on Friday that 50 people had been killed and more than 500 injured in the current round of fighting. The latest tit-for-tat attacks came as the region lurched back towards a total war despite an interim peace deal agreed last month. The memorandum of understanding (MoU) to halt attacks and open the strait is more than halfway through a 60-day implementation period that was intended to provide breathing room for diplomacy. Shipping in the strait has slowed to a trickle as a result of the fighting and the price of oil reached its highest in a month, passing $90 (£67) a barrel, exacerbating the global energy crisis. A Houthi blockade of Saudi ports announced on Monday could further strain global energy markets as Saudi Arabia was using the Red Sea route to export millions of barrels of oil and circumvent Iran’s closure of the strait of Hormuz. The group said it was responding to what it called Riyadh’s targeting of Sana’a airport last week after it was bombed by the Saudi-backed Yemen government, and that it would answer a “blockade with a blockade”. But in a sign that talks could still stop the escalating round of conflict, an Iranian official told Reuters that mediators gave Iran a proposal for a 10-day-ceasefire to find ways to revive the MoU. The proposal follows a statement by Iran that diplomacy with the US was continuing despite the fighting, without giving details on the content of the discussions. Iran’s interior minister was expected to travel to Islamabad on Monday, Agence France-Presse reported. Hours earlier, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said the US was still open to negotiations with Iran, but that “it has to be real”. “We’re going to continue to respond. If the door opens to diplomacy – if the guys that want to do something productive for Iran win and take control of that system, or take control of the negotiations – that’ll be a very positive development,” Rubio said. View image in fullscreen A bridge hit by a US strike in Iran’s Hormozgan province, pictured on Sunday. Photograph: Anadolu/Getty Images Mediators including Qatar and Pakistan have continued to speak to both sides during the current round of escalation in an attempt to bring the two sides back to the negotiating table. As the strikes began early on Monday, a ship caught fire in the strait of Hormuz near the coastline of Oman in the southern corridor the US military has encouraged ships to use to avoid Iran’s control. The crew safely abandoned the vessel, while the ship was recovered by a tug boat, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed two oil tankers were blown up after attempting to transit through the southern route, but there was no independent confirmation. The IRGC also claimed to have launched a “surprise attack” on an enemy command centre in the al-Tanf region of Syria, in a statement reported by the state news agency IRNA. After Iran targeted sites in Jordan on Sunday, Israel warned of the danger of the strikes spilling across its borders, reigniting a direct regional conflict that had been restrained even as tensions between the Trump administration and Iran have soared again. “If Iran fires missiles at Israel, we will attack them with full force,” said the Israeli defence minister, Israel Katz, on Sunday. The US is expected to send dozens of additional refuelling planes to Israel as part of a reinforcement of its force posture in the region, a US official told Reuters. Trump has threatened to target Iran’s power stations and bridges to try to compel Tehran to loosen its hold on the strait of Hormuz. View image in fullscreen A vessel in the strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman, on 17 July. Photograph: Reuters The US energy secretary, Chris Wright, defended the Trump administration’s strategy for the war, but argued that the mission had since shifted toward ensuring oil shipments could move through the strait. “We did a pause to try to get an agreement with the Iranian people to allow oil to flow without them attacking it,” Wright said on Sunday. “They’ve proven unwilling to do that, and we’ve pivoted. Now what we’re doing is assuring that that oil and gas and other products can flow through the strait of Hormuz with or without Iranian cooperation.” The US also announced that the “pilot zone” scheme in Lebanon had begun in three villages in the south, after peace talks in Rome between the governments of Lebanon and Israel. Under the programme, Israel will withdraw soldiers from areas it occupies in south Lebanon, to be replaced by the Lebanese army, which will be tasked with dismantling Hezbollah infrastructure in the areas. The US state department spokesperson Thomas Pigott said on Monday that the scheme started in the villages of Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya. The Associated Press contributed reporting
centerLeftText: U.S. launches 10th straight night of strikes on Iran as Pentagon reports nearly 100 injuries in July The U.S. on Monday launched its 10th straight night of strikes on Iran, CENTCOM said. The news comes as the Pentagon confirms nearly 100 U.S. service members were hurt in Iranian airstrikes on multiple bases this month. CBS News' Charlie D'Agata and Aaron MacLean have the latest.

topic: Maine Replace Graham Platner Progressive Frontrunner

left.title: Progressive frontrunner and Maine logger poised to replace Graham Platner in chaotic primary

left-center.title: Maine Democrat Shenna Bellows drops out of race to replace Graham Platner

center.title: Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner

farLeftText: Troy Jackson – a logger, longtime legislator and former president of the Maine senate – is now poised to become the Democratic nominee in Maine’s US Senate race. His rise comes after the party’s previous nominee, Graham Platner, abruptly ended his campaign earlier this month following allegations of sexual assault from a former partner. Platner’s withdrawal set off a frantic scramble inside the Maine Democratic party. Several contenders filed paperwork to be considered at a 25 July special convention of 601 delegates tasked with selecting a new nominee. But over the weekend, Jackson’s serious challengers dropped out one after another, clearing a swift and unobstructed path for him. Jackson, who lost the Democratic gubernatorial primary earlier this year, is now the presumptive choice. Among those who ended their bids was Jordan Wood, a former congressional staffer who ran unsuccessfully in the Democratic primary for Maine’s second district. Two other candidates who also fell short in this year’s gubernatorial primary – former Maine CDC director Nirav Shah and current secretary of state Shenna Bellows – likewise ended their campaigns. Dan Kleban, founder of Maine Beer Company, who briefly ran in the Senate primary against Platner before withdrawing months ago, also announced he would not stay in the race. Wood, Shah and Kleban have all endorsed Jackson, stressing the stakes of flipping the seat held for nearly three decades by Republican Susan Collins – the only GOP senator running for re-election in a state Kamala Harris carried in 2024. “This fight is bigger than any one person,” Wood said in a social media video. “There is too much at stake in our country, our democracy – our state is depending on us.” Maine is crucial to Democrats’ effort to net the four seats required to retake control of the Senate in November’s midterms. Over the weekend, all 16 counties elected 500 delegates who will vote in Bangor on Saturday, with the remaining 101 delegates coming from the state party’s central committee. Jackson, now the progressive frontrunner, once campaigned alongside Platner and received his endorsement during his gubernatorial run, where he finished third, which raised concerns that Jackson might be tainted in the breakneck process to replace the disgraced former nominee. Questions have also lingered about Jackson’s public speaking style and whether he could match the commanding presence that helped Platner build a statewide following for his populist platform. But Jackson has consolidated support from major unions, numerous state Democrats and national progressive groups – he also appears to have locked down broad backing from the newly selected delegates. While their commitments are not legally binding, a majority of the 601 delegates have now pledged to vote for him, according to Jackson’s campaign. “Those delegates were not handed to us. You know, we earned them because hundreds and hundreds of people organized their neighbors, made the calls, had the conversations,” Jackson said in Sanford. On Sunday, Jackson also told Politico it was “incredible” to see people “really concerned about making sure this momentum, this movement continues” as it became clear he would emerge as the presumptive nominee. His campaign said on Monday it was turning its “full focus” to defeating Collins after the weekend’s surge of delegate support. State Democrats have until 27 July to formally choose Platner’s replacement for the general election ballot. Jackson, 58, was first elected to the legislature in 2002 and became senate president in 2018. He left office in 2024 due to term limits.

topic: Sentenced Life Prison Drug Mayo Cartel

left.title: Mexican drug lord ‘El Mayo’ sentenced to life in prison in US federal court

center.title: Notorious drug cartel leader Ismael Zambada sentenced to life in US prison

right.title: Sinaloa Cartel Kingpin 'El Mayo' Sentenced in U.S. to Life in Prison

farLeftText: The Mexican drug kingpin, Ismael Zambada García, known as “El Mayo”, was sentenced in a US federal court on Monday to life in prison, after a plea deal last year in which he admitted ⁠to shipping vast amounts of cocaine and other substances as leader of the violent Sinaloa cartel. Zambada is 76 and one of the most high-profile Mexican ⁠drug traffickers to be sentenced in a US court since his fellow Sinaloa leader Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán ​was sentenced to life in prison in ‌2019. The case unfolded in federal court in Brooklyn, New York. Zambada helped co-found and lead the cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades. He signed a plea deal last August, admitting charges including racketeering conspiracy and continuing criminal enterprise, after the ‌US justice department said it would not seek the death penalty. The court also ordered him to forfeit $15bn from drug trade profits as part of his punishment. As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. He was accused of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world. Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents. Zambada, who has spent nearly two years inside a Brooklyn federal prison, limped inside the courtroom. He was wearing a beige prison outfit and had white hair and a beard. Before his sentencing, Zambada read a public letter, saying that he took responsibility for his crimes and calling for an end to the violence that has plagued Mexico for decades during the drug-fueled conflict. “I did not go to trial because I did not want to deny the truth,” Zambada read aloud in court, adding that “violence must end” and urging the next generation of drug traffickers to “choose a different path”. While El Chapo is better known as the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly El Mayo was regarded as the elusive, behind the scenes leader running the operations, with a low profile and few public sightings. El Mayo’s arrest in the US in 2024 came as a surprise. During a mysterious operation, he was known to have been beaten and kidnapped by one of El Chapo’s sons, Joaquín Guzmán López, and was flown across the US border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents. It remains unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. Guzmán López pled guilty in an abduction case late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US justice department. El Mayo’s arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El Mayo’s son and El Chapo’s sons leading different factions, in a raging intra-cartel battle for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico. El Mayo’s sentencing comes at a tense time in US-Mexico relations, as the Trump administration has escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to crack down harder on criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened US military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug-trafficking organizations. US district judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El Chapo’s trial, has been hearing the case in New York. Cogan acknowledged that the sentencing hearing was “anticlimactic” because of the plea agreement. All parties involved understood that Zambada would spend the rest of his life in prison, the judge said. Frank Perez, a lawyer for El Mayo, ​had said in a 6 July court filing that his client was fully aware he would receive a life sentence when he decided to accept responsibility for his crimes. Perez asked Cogan to recommend to the US Bureau of Prisons that his client, as a septuagenarian, be placed in a ⁠facility where he would receive adequate medical care. Prosecutors with the Brooklyn US attorney’s office said the prisons ​suggested by Perez may not ​be equipped to handle the security concerns posed by Zambada, including the ​possibility that he could ‌continue to direct ​cartel activities from ​behind bars. “Those security concerns will continue for the foreseeable future because individuals loyal to the defendant continue to control the cartel,” the prosecutors wrote in a 13 July filing. Zambada’s defense attorneys on Monday requested that the judge recommend a federal medical facility, rather than a traditional prison, due to his medical conditions. Health details are redacted in court records, but the judge mentioned during the sentencing hearing that Zambada is “drifting” towards being “cognitively impaired”. Mexican outlets have reported Zambada is dealing with the early stages of dementia. The Bureau of Prisons still has to evaluate where Zambada will be sent to serve the rest of his days. El Chapo is serving his life sentence at a maximum-security prison in Colorado. Frank Perez, one of Zambada’s attorneys, said during the sentencing hearing that his client did not cooperate with the US government, neither providing information to US officials about other cartel members or leaders, nor Mexican officials who have protected the cartel’s drug-trafficking business. Reuters contributed reporting

centerText: One of the most notorious figures in Mexico's drug cartels, Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada, will spend the rest of his life in prison, a US judge has ruled, two years after the kingpin was lured to the US in an elaborate sting operation. The 76-year-old, who founded the Sinaloa Cartel with Joaquin "El Chapo" Guzmán, apologised in court and was also ordered to pay $15bn (£11bn). In 2024, El Chapo's son led Zambada to believe they were travelling to northern Mexico to inspect clandestine airstrips, when their plane instead landed in Texas and he was arrested. Last year, Zambada reached a plea deal and admitted to drug smuggling and conspiracy charges in exchange for avoiding the death penalty. On Monday, US District Judge Brian Cogan sentenced Zambada during a hearing in Brooklyn federal court. His guilty plea carried a mandatory term of life in prison. Zambada spoke through a Spanish translator during the hearing. He said he was sorry "for the harm I caused and for the example I set" and called for future generations to "choose a different path". "If there's one thing I can say that has value today, it's this: the violence must end," said the former cartel boss. "In Mexico and elsewhere affected by this type of crime, too many lives are lost, too many families are destroyed, and too many young people are trapped in a cycle that leads only to prison or death." The $15bn he was ordered to pay represents drug-trafficking profits from his role as the principal leader of a criminal enterprise, according to the justice department. El Chapo's son, Joaquin Guzmán Lopez, was also arrested in July 2024 and has pleaded guilty to federal drug charges. He has yet to be sentenced. In the plea deal, Zambada officially accepted his role in creating the vast criminal network which has sent huge amounts of cocaine and other drugs into the US since he co-founded the Sinaloa Cartel at the end of the 1980s. "Ismael Zambada Garcia spent nearly four decades poisoning American communities to make billions of dollars in profit and ordering the murders of anyone who stood in his way," said US Attorney Joseph Nocella Jr for the Eastern District of New York in a statement. "Today, that chapter closes for good." Prosecutors argued in a sentencing memo that Zambada "was one of the most-if not the most-prolific and powerful narcotraffickers in the world", according to CBS, the BBC's US partner. The Sinaloa Cartel has been at war with itself since Zambada was taken into custody and has wreaked mayhem across the Mexican state of Sinaloa. Hundreds of people have been reported missing during fighting between its two rival factions. Violence and bloodletting also shook the cartel after El Chapo was arrested and extradited to the US in 2016 as factions vied for control of territory while fighting off opposing drug gangs who sensed weakness. He is also serving life in prison. One of the issues that has arisen since Zambada agreed to plead guilty has been where he will serve his sentence. He has asked to be sent to a prison hospital, saying he will need medical care for age-related health issues. He is understood to have been in poor health in recent years. Federal prosecutors, though, are seeking a higher-level of incarceration, contending Zambada still poses a security threat while behind bars and could continue to direct illegal drug operations. The federal Bureau of Prisons will make the final decision about where he is held, though he looks almost certain to die in US custody. On his first day back in office, US President Donald Trump announced he would increase pressure on Mexico and the cartels, including designating the criminal enterprises terrorist groups. The US also accused the governor of Sinaloa of conspiring with drug traffickers. The Mexican government, too, has taken more aggressive steps against cartels and sent thousands of troops to Sinaloa. The question of what Zambada's punishment means to the Sinaloa cartel is unclear. In all probability, it will not change the status quo. Organised crime moves on quickly in Mexico and El Mayo's former footsoldiers have already adapted to life without him. Neither is it likely to significantly affect the traffic of cocaine and fentanyl into the US. There is always another ready to take over the mantle, even from so influential a crime boss as El Mayo Zambada.

farRightText: The maximum leader of Mexico’s most powerful drug cartel, the terrorist organization known as the Sinaloa Cartel, will spend the rest of his life in prison. The kingpin and his organization are linked directly to the ongoing fentanyl crisis that has been plaguing the United States On Monday morning, Ismael “El Mayo” Zambada went before U.S. District Judge Brian Cogan, who handed down the life sentence. The sentence was anticipated ahead of time following his August 2025 guilty plea, where the Mexican drug lord admitted to multiple drug trafficking conspiracy charges linked to his role as one of the founders and top leaders of the Sinaloa Cartel. According to the U.S. Department of Justice, the Sinaloa Cartel is responsible for moving tons of cocaine, methamphetamine, and fentanyl into the United States. Additionally, the criminal organization has a long history of extreme violence being directly linked to numerous murders, mass killings, forced disappearances, and other high-level crimes. As Breitbart Texas has reported, Zambada’s arrest sparked a political storm and a fierce turf war that split the Sinaloa Cartel in two. During the summer of 2024, members of the Los Chapitos faction of the Sinaloa Cartel called a meeting with El Mayo and some high-level politicians only to kidnap the drug lord and fly him to the United States and turn him over to authorities. The operation fueled tensions between the United States and Mexico. Since the start of the Trump Administration, the U.S. government has been pressuring Mexico to fight drug cartels. Earlier this year, tensions between the two countries escalated when the U.S. Department of Justice criminally indicted the current governor of Sinaloa, Rubén Rocha Moya, and nine of his closest allies for allegedly working with the Sinaloa Cartel. Rather than assist the U.S. with arrests and extradition, Sheinbaum has publicly defended Rocha Moya, claiming that there is no proof of wrongdoing against the politician. The strikes - the 10th consecutive night of attacks - come after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers, saying it would pay "many times over". At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire. Earlier in the day, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones. Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected. On the US strikes the previous night, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that several cities had been hit including Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini. Iranian state media reported that two oil tankers had exploded as the vessels tried to cross the strait. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the tankers were going through what it called an "unsafe and hazardous southern route" of the strait and "exploded and were brought to a halt". Centcom disputed the claims.

topic: Troy Jackson Maine Senate Effectively Secured

left-center.title: Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Maine Senate Democratic nomination

center.title: Troy Jackson emerges as likely Senate pick for Maine Democrats

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: Troy Jackson, a former Maine lawmaker and fifth-generation logger from the North Maine Woods, has emerged as the likely new replacement for the state's Democratic Senate nominee after former candidate Graham Platner quit the high-stakes race less than two weeks ago. Platner's departure followed a sexual assault allegation that he denied but forced his exit anyway. It sparked a wave of potential candidates eager to prove to Mainers that they could take on longtime Republican Sen. Susan Collins. But by Sunday, the field had largely been cleared after Jackson supporters spent the weekend securing hundreds of delegates who promised to back him at the upcoming state convention. Under a never-before-used system to replace Platner, delegates will vote Saturday in Bangor on a new Senate nominee. Out of the 601 delegates who will gather this weekend, Jackson's campaign says they have 481 delegates committed to supporting him. Top opponents — ranging from Secretary of State Shenna Bellows to Nirav Shah, former director of Maine's Center for Disease Control and Prevention — have since dropped out, while Jackson announced Monday that he was focusing his attention on ousting Collins. "I'm grateful to Democrats across Maine for putting their faith in me and in what we're fighting for," Jackson said in a statement. "This didn't happen because of one person. It happened because hundreds of volunteers put in the hours, knocked on doors, made phone calls, and believed we could build something bigger than ourselves." There are challenges ahead for Jackson in a race with Collins Yet securing the nomination is only just the beginning of challenges facing Jackson. There's little more than 100 days until the general election, meaning Jackson faces an uphill battle raising campaign funds when Collins already has millions. Jackson also must implement a statewide campaign network and woo over the state's large independent base. As former state Senate president, Jackson may be more politically vetted than Platner was running as a first-time candidate, but the logger's backing of progressive policies will leave him open to plenty of attacks by Republicans, said Nicholas Jacobs, a political scientist at Colby College in Maine. "The guy does have some pretty interesting positions," Jacobs said. "Positions that are pretty far to the left of the median voter." READ MORE: Maine Democrats criticize GOP Sen. Susan Collins after fatal ICE shooting of motorist in the state Jackson supports 'Medicare for All' and eliminating ICE Like Platner, Jackson has said he wants to "get rid of" Immigration and Customs Enforcement, which has become top of mind for many Mainers after an ICE officer fatally shot a driver last week. Democrats have rushed to connect Collins to the embattled federal agency, while Jackson has reiterated his support of dissolving ICE altogether. Jackson has also called for "Medicare for All," which would replace job-based and individual private health insurance with a government-run plan that guarantees coverage for all with no premiums, no deductibles and only minimal copays for certain services. Republicans were already criticizing Jackson as a "socialist" on Monday. "This entire process is just as bad as Kamala's rigged coronation," said Republican National Committee spokesperson Kristen Cianci, nodding to former President Joe Biden's decision to drop out of the presidential race in July 2024, leaving Vice President Kamala Harris to head up the Democratic ticket and ultimately lose to Republican Donald Trump. "Maine voters have had their voices stolen by party insiders," Cianci added. Jackson first ran for office as a Republican However, unlike the scrutiny Platner faced from a series of controversies, Jackson's background coming from "old Maine" and his decades of working in the logging industry will help him, Jacobs said. "He's the real deal," Jacobs said. In 1998, Jackson helped lead a weeklong blockade along the Quebec border in an effort to protest the hiring of Canadian loggers who were coming into Maine and driving down pay rates. The standoff ended with Jackson being escorted away by police, but resulted in Jackson fighting for better logger and wood hauler protections as a state lawmaker. Jackson initially ran as a Republican in 2000 when he sought a seat inside Maine's House of Representatives. He lost, but won two years later when he ran as an independent. He switched to the Democratic Party in 2004, and eventually became the Maine Senate president before leaving the Statehouse in 2024. He came in third while running for governor in this year's Democratic primary election, but had the backing of Platner, U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders and Our Revolution, the political organization founded by Sanders. Democrats must net four Senate seats to gain control of the 100-member chamber, and party leaders viewed Maine as a critical piece of the puzzle, along with Alaska, Ohio and North Carolina.

left.title: Trump says Benjamin Netanyahu ‘will not be arrested’ if he visits the US

left-center.title: Trump Says Netanyahu Will Not Be Arrested in the U.S.

farLeftText: Donald Trump has said Benjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli prime minister, “will not be arrested” if he visits the US. His comments came days after Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, said he was exploring whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu if he visits the city during the UN general assembly in September. In a Truth Social post on Monday, Trump said Netanyahu, who has been the subject of an international criminal court arrest warrant since November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form while in the United States of America”. Trump added that Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and said “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Speaking to the New York Times on Saturday, Mamdani had said: “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” where the international criminal court is based, adding: “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.” Mamdani, who has described Israel’s war on Gaza as a genocide, also said he is in “an active conversation” with New York City’s law department about his legal authority to direct the New York police to detain Netanyahu. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” he said, adding: “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.” Although the US does not recognize the ICC’s jurisdiction and is not a party to the court, Mamdani pledged during his mayoral campaign last September that he would arrest Netanyahu if he traveled to New York. “It is my desire to ensure that this be a city that stands up for international law,” he said. Other Israeli leaders, including Yoav Gallant, the country’s former defense minister, as well as the Hamas military leader Mohammed Deif, have also been the subject of ICC arrest warrants. The court accused the Israeli leaders of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”.

topic: Thousands Cockroach Protesters Police Tens Movement

left.title: Tens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi march

center.title: Police block thousands of 'cockroach' protesters marching on India's parliament

farLeftText: Tens of thousands of protesters have defied police orders and descended on Delhi to demand the resignation of India’s education minister in one of the largest anti-government demonstrations in years. A huge crowd, largely made up of students and gen Z-ers, filled the streets in the heart of India’s capital from early Monday morning shouting anti-government slogans. Hundreds had already camped out overnight. Delhi police, which are under the control of the central government, had denied permission for the march to take place. A ban was imposed on all public gatherings, roads across the city were barricaded and the internet was shut down in the centre. Attempts by protesters to move towards the parliament building were met with police brutality including the use of batons and teargas. However, this did little to prevent protesters turning out in their thousands, many of whom were vocal in their anger at Narendra Modi’s government and his ruling Bharatiya Janata party (BJP), accusing them of failing the country’s young people. “They are calling this protest illegal, they are calling us anti-national,” said Rhythm Katoch. “But I am 22 years old and I am standing here to fight for our education system, for the rights of the young people and for our democracy. Is it too much to ask for the government to listen to us?” View image in fullscreen The protesters were vocal in their anger at Narendra Modi’s government and his ruling Bharatiya Janata party. Photograph: Shekhar Yadav/AP A call had been made to march on parliament by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), a new youth-led movement whose name was inspired by remarks made in May by Surya Kant, the chief justice of India, when he called some activists and unemployed youth “cockroaches”. While the CJP began as satire, founded by the graduate Abhijeet Dipke in May, it has swiftly mobilised into a serious political challenge to the Modi government. Its core demand has been the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, over exam chaos, after this year’s medical entrance exam – where more than 2 million students compete for just 130,000 places – was again leaked to the highest bidder, forcing it to be scrapped and for students to face retaking the gruelling tests. The fiasco has been linked to more than 20 suicides. Wider frustrations over alleged failures in the education system and a lack of job opportunities for India’s youth have also mobilised support. View image in fullscreen CJP supporters clash with police during the march. Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty Images Rage was palpable on Delhi’s streets on Monday. Harion Sharma, 18, was among those forced to retake the medical exam last month after it was leaked, which he said had put “unbearable” pressure on him and his fellow students. “This is a government that does not care about education, it does not care about the pressures and pain of young people. Because of those leaks, students have died. So we are here to call for accountability and for the education minister to resign. We are young but we are powerful.” Sharma described the high turnout of the protest as historic. “They might be trying to crush our futures and our democracy, but look around, how young people have come to the streets in such massive numbers,” he added. “Now, finally, the government is scared of us.” The use of police force against the protesters was met with condemnation by opposition politicians. Speaking outside parliament, Priyanka Gandhi, a leader of the opposition Congress party, said: “There are massive issues … but you are firing teargas at children and beating them up? For what?” For weeks, the government had ignored the CJP supporters camped out less than a mile from parliament. Pradhan had sought to dismiss their calls for his resignation by calling them “terrorists”. However, after the huge turnout on Monday, the Modi government invited several CJP spokespeople for talks. In a social media post, the CJP spokesperson Saurav Das said they had waited two hours and were given a brief meeting with a cabinet minister, JP Nadda. Das said the government had made no commitments so far. View image in fullscreen The renowned engineer and education activist Sonam Wangchuk has been on hunger strike in solidarity with the students. Photograph: Reuters While neighbouring countries such as Nepal, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka have had governments toppled by youth-led movements, such large-scale expressions of dissent have become unusual in India in recent years. The BJP, which has ruled for 12 years under Modi, has amassed unprecedented power at national and state level while also routinely jailing and persecuting its critics and opponents, leaving little room for dissent. The BJP MP Kangana Ranaut was among the ruling government politicians to criticise the protest, telling reporters that “no one can pressure the government into who to sack and who to retain”. The CJP protest had been galvanised by a hunger strike carried out by the renowned engineer and education activist Sonam Wangchuk, who in solidarity with the students has refused all food and water for the past 21 days until Pradhan agrees to resign. View image in fullscreen A police officer fires teargas shells towards the protesters. Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty Images Nationwide outrage grew after the Delhi police arrived early on Sunday morning and forcibly removed Wangchuk against his will, with footage of the incident going viral. Wangchuk was taken to a government hospital where he claimed he was being “held hostage”. In a statement, his wife, Gitanjali Angmo, condemned the heavy police presence around Wangchuk in the hospital ward. “It is not medical care,” she said. “It is illegal detention.” In response, organisers of the CJP also began a hunger strike and called on supporters to join Monday’s march, which Wangchuk said would be “India’s second freedom movement”. Amrita, 27, said she had taken a day off work to join the protest, calling the government’s treatment of Wangchuk “shameless”. “We have a coward government who is afraid of the young people and afraid of Sonam Wangchuk,” she said. “He is a brilliant brain and he has put his life on the line for young people. How he is being treated is a shame on India. That’s why the gen Zs have come out today to speak out.” By Monday evening there had been no formal response by Pradhan to the calls for his resignation and CJP organisers said they would remain at the protest site. In a handwritten note from hospital, Wangchuk said that “seeing the brutality with which peacefully protesting youth are being dealt with, I have decided to continue my fast”.

centerText:
It gathered fresh momentum over the weekend after police forcibly moved activist Sonam Wangchuk into hospital on the 21st day of a hunger strike in support of the movement. His wife says his fast continues. On Monday, Wangchuk appealed to doctors to discharge him so he could join the march but was not allowed.

The movement takes its name from a remark by India's chief justice, who drew criticism after comparing sections of unemployed young people to "cockroaches" during a court hearing.

Although police had made clear protesters would not be given permission to march to parliament, tens of thousands of students, parents, teachers and professionals gathered from early morning at Jantar Mantar, a 300-year-old observatory in central Delhi, and remained there despite heavy rain.

It remains unclear whether the CJP will call off its protest or try to continue the sit-in. By late evening police had completely dismantled the protest site.

The contrast between it and the security outside was striking. Young people draped in Indian flags queued for food, painted placards and posed for photographs, while rows of police and paramilitary personnel sealed every road to parliament around them.

Volunteers handed out food and water, patriotic songs blared from loudspeakers and organisers repeatedly urged supporters not to confront police if they were stopped.
